Swiss CitiesBern

Bern

BEBern

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The bear who founded Bern

The founding legend of Bern retold from the bear cub's perspective. A young bear befriends Duke Berchtold V in the forest and shows him the perfect peninsula where the Aare bends -- the place that will become one of Europe's most beautiful medieval cities, forever bearing the bear's name.

Create This Story

In 1191, Duke Berchtold V of Zaehringen founded the city of Bern. According to the founding legend, the duke declared the city would be named after the first animal killed in the surrounding forest during a hunt -- that animal was a bear (Baer). The more likely etymology is from the Celtic word 'berna' meaning cleft or gorge, referring to the dramatic Aare peninsula. Bern sits on an elevated peninsula formed by the Aare river bending sharply, creating a natural fortress surrounded by water on three sides.

Time travel with the Zytglogge

When Hans von Thann accidentally strikes 13, a time portal opens in the Zytglogge tower. A child steps through and visits three eras of Bern's history: 1530 (meeting clockmaker Kaspar Brunner), 1405 (escaping the great fire), and 1200 (watching the city gate being built). They must return before the 13th chime fades.

Create This Story

The Zytglogge was built around 1200 as the western city gate. In 1530, locksmith Kaspar Brunner completed an astronomical clockwork for 1,000 Gulden -- one of the oldest functioning mechanical clocks in Switzerland. It shows hours, minutes, day, date, month, zodiac signs, moon phases, sunrise, and sunset. Animated figures perform every hour: the Golden Rooster crows three times, Chronos (nicknamed 'Hans von Thann') turns an hourglass, and dancing bears in Bern's red and black colours were added in 1610.

Einstein and the light that wouldn't wait

A child discovers Einstein's forgotten notebook in the apartment at Kramgasse 49 and rides a beam of light past frozen Bern landmarks -- the Zytglogge, the Muenster, the Aare. As the light races away from the clock tower, time itself bends, and the child understands why one patent clerk's spare-time scribbles changed the universe.

Create This Story

Einstein moved to Bern in 1902 to work at the Swiss Federal Patent Office as 'Technical Expert, Third Class.' He and his wife Mileva Maric rented an apartment at Kramgasse 49 in 1903, directly overlooking the Zytglogge clock tower. Between March and September 1905, he published five groundbreaking papers including the photoelectric effect (Nobel Prize 1921), Brownian motion, and special relativity (E=mc2) -- all written in his spare time after full days at the patent office.

The dancing bears of the clock tower

One of the mechanical dancing bears on the Zytglogge gets stuck and cannot join the hourly dance. A child must climb inside the ancient clockwork to fix the jammed gears -- discovering Kaspar Brunner's 1530 masterpiece from within -- so that all the bears can dance together once more.

Create This Story

The Zytglogge's dancing bears were added in 1610, eighty years after Kaspar Brunner built the clockwork. The bears are dressed in Bern's colours (red and black) and dance in a circle at every hour, symbolizing the city's power and honouring the founding legend. Bears are everywhere in Bern: on the cantonal coat of arms, on fountains, as chocolate bears in shops, and in the BaerenPark -- a 6,000 square metre park opened in 2009 where real bears roam freely on the Aare riverbank.

The secret of the Kindlifresser

A child follows clues from fountain to fountain across Bern's old town, visiting all 11 of Hans Gieng's historic creations. Each fountain whispers a fragment of the Kindlifresser's true identity. At the end of the trail, the child discovers that the fearsome 'ogre' was really a protector all along -- guarding a secret beneath the Kornhausplatz.

Create This Story

The Kindlifresserbrunnen (Child Eater Fountain) at Kornhausplatz was created in 1545-1546 by sculptor Hans Gieng. It depicts a seated ogre devouring a naked child, with more children in a bag at his side. Its true meaning is one of Bern's great mysteries -- theories include the Greek Titan Kronos, a medieval bogeyman, an anti-Semitic caricature, a carnival figure, or a political allegory. Bern has over 100 public fountains, with 11 historic ones created by Hans Gieng in the 1540s.

Swimming with the Aare

A nervous child takes their first Aareschwimmen, guided by a friendly otter who knows every current and eddy. Together they float past the bears in the BaerenPark, under the ancient bridges, and alongside the Bundeshaus -- discovering that the river that once made Bern a fortress now makes it a playground.

Create This Story

The Aare drops about 30 meters in steep cliffs below Bern's old town, encircling the city like a protective belt. Aare swimming (Aareschwimmen/Aareboetle) is a beloved summer tradition where locals float down the turquoise river in inflatable rafts. The modern BaerenPark, opened on 22 October 2009, is a 6,000 square metre riverside park where bears can roam freely. The Bundeshaus (Federal Palace), built in 1902, houses the Swiss parliament on the Aare's high bank.

The Muenster's 500 steps

A child climbs the 344 steps of the Berner Muenster tower, and at each landing a different century of Bern's history comes alive -- from the first stone laid in 1421 to the tower's completion in 1893. At the top, the 234 figures of the Last Judgment portal wave goodbye as the entire panorama of the Bernese Alps unfolds.

Create This Story

The Berner Muenster is Switzerland's largest late-medieval church and its tallest cathedral at 100.6 metres. Construction began in 1421 under Matthaeus Ensinger from Strasbourg and was only completed in 1893 -- nearly 500 years later. A 344-step spiral staircase leads to the top for panoramic views. The main entrance features the Last Judgment portal with 234 human figures from all ranks of society, created by Erhard Kueng -- one of the most important Late Gothic sculptural programs in Europe.

The lost bear of 1513

A bear captured at the Battle of Novara in 1513 escapes from its handlers on the way to Bern. A child must find the runaway bear across medieval Bern -- through the Lauben arcades, past the Zytglogge, over the Aare bridges -- before Bartholomaeus May's grand procession begins at the city gates.

Create This Story

In 1513, Swiss mercenaries captured a live bear after the Battle of Novara in Northern Italy. Bartholomaeus May, Bern's most prominent citizen, led the captured bear ceremoniously into the city. In 1515, the first bear pit (Baerengraben) opened at the current Baerenplatz. The bear became the living embodiment of the city's name and coat of arms. Bears have been kept in Bern continuously since then, moving through multiple locations before the modern BaerenPark opened in 2009.
